methodologies.name: Spec-Driven Development
methodologies.summary: Write the spec first and treat it as the source of truth; the code is what the agent generates from it. Start with a short constitution of standing principles, then specify what to build, plan how to build it, break the plan into agent-sized tasks, and only then let the agent implement. When requirements change you change the spec, not the code, and regenerate.
methodologies.intent: Make the specification, not the code, the source of truth so an AI agent implements against a settled, reviewable description.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this when an AI coding agent does the bulk of the implementation and you want control over what it builds. It fits greenfield features and well-scoped changes where the requirements can be pinned down up front. Don't apply it to pure exploration where you do not yet know what you want.

inputs.name: Feature idea
inputs.description: The thing to build, stated as an outcome a user wants, not as code.

inputs.name: Project constitution
inputs.description: The standing principles and constraints the project always honours: stack, style, non-negotiables.

inputs.name: Spec-driven tooling
inputs.description: A toolkit such as Spec Kit and an AI coding agent that can read specs, plans, and tasks.

outputs.name: Feature specification
outputs.description: A reviewed document of what to build, for whom, why, and the core flows, free of implementation detail.

outputs.name: Technical plan
outputs.description: How the feature will be built: stack choices, architecture, and constraints derived from the spec.

outputs.name: Task list
outputs.description: The plan broken into discrete, agent-executable tasks.

outputs.name: Implementation
outputs.description: Code the agent generates from the tasks, which serves the spec rather than the other way around.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  idea[Feature idea] --> con[Write constitution]
  con --> spec[Specify: what, who, why, flows]
  spec --> plan[Plan: stack + architecture]
  plan --> tasks[Break into agent-sized tasks]
  tasks --> impl[Agent implements from tasks]
  impl --> change{Requirements change?}
  change -->|yes| spec
  change -->|no| done[Shipped feature serving the spec]

methodologies.example_scenario: A small team adds a saved-search feature to an analytics product, with an AI coding agent doing most of the typing. Their old habit was to prompt the agent straight into code, which produced plausible files that missed half the edge cases. They switched to spec-driven development with Spec Kit. First they wrote a one-page constitution: TypeScript, their existing auth, no new datastore without sign-off. Then they specified the feature: who saves searches, what a saved search contains, how sharing works, and the empty and error states, all without naming a single function. They reviewed the spec and caught a missing rule about who can delete a shared search before any code existed. The agent then produced a plan that reused the existing search index, which they approved, and broke it into nine tasks. The agent implemented task by task. When product later changed sharing from per-user to per-team, they edited the spec and regenerated the affected tasks instead of hand-patching code, and the change stayed consistent across the feature.
methodologies.deep_dive: The core move is an inversion: code is the generated output and the spec is the source of truth, so the spec must be precise enough that an agent can build from it. Spec Kit ships the process as four ordered phases on top of a constitution: Specify (what and why, no how), Plan (the how, given the stack), Tasks (plan split into agent-sized units), and Implement (the agent writes code to satisfy the settled tasks). The constitution is the durable layer; it records principles that every feature honours so they do not have to be restated each time. The discipline pays off twice: review happens against a readable spec while change is still cheap, and later edits are made to the spec and regenerated rather than smeared across hand-written code. The main failure mode is a spec that quietly encodes implementation choices, which removes the agent's room to plan and reintroduces the coupling the method is meant to avoid. Keep the spec about behaviour; let the plan own the how. It pairs with the catalog patterns `spec-first-agent` and `spec-driven-loop`, which describe the per-run agent shape this methodology operationalises across a project.

steps.name: Write the constitution
steps.description: Record the standing principles the project always honours: stack, style, and non-negotiable constraints. This layer outlives any one feature.

steps.name: Specify the feature
steps.description: Describe what to build, who it is for, what problem it solves, and the core and edge flows. State behaviour, not implementation.

steps.name: Plan the technical approach
steps.description: Turn the spec into a plan: stack choices, architecture, and constraints. This is where the how lives.

steps.name: Break into agent-sized tasks
steps.description: Split the plan into discrete tasks an agent can pick up and finish, each with a clear done state.

steps.name: Let the agent implement
steps.description: The agent writes code to satisfy the tasks, plan, and spec that are already settled.

steps.name: Re-spec on change
steps.description: When requirements change, edit the spec and regenerate the affected tasks rather than hand-patching the generated code.

principles: The spec is the source of truth; code is its generated output.
Specify behaviour, not implementation; let the plan own the how.
Review while change is cheap, against a readable spec.
On change, edit the spec and regenerate, do not patch the code.

known_failure_modes.note: Writing a spec for a process that is itself broken just makes the agent build the broken thing faster.
Letting the agent implement from tasks with no review gate reintroduces the unreviewed, unsafe code the spec was meant to prevent.

sources.title: github/spec-kit
sources.quote: Spec-Driven Development ... the specifications as the source of truth, with code as the generated output that serves the specification.

sources.title: Diving Into Spec-Driven Development With GitHub Spec Kit — Microsoft for Developers

methodologies.name: Requirements-First Spec Workflow
methodologies.summary: Drive the build from a structured requirements document, then a design, then tasks. Write requirements as user stories with EARS acceptance criteria of the form 'when X, the system shall Y'. Generate a design with architecture and data models from those requirements. Break the design into tasks the agent runs, independent ones first. A design-first variant flips the order when the architecture is the hard part.
methodologies.intent: Formalise a feature as testable requirements, then a validated design, then dependency-ordered tasks an agent can execute.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this when a feature is complex enough that jumping to code loses requirements, and when you want acceptance criteria you can check implementation against. It suits regulated or interface-heavy work where 'the system shall' statements matter. Don't apply the requirements-first order when the architecture is the genuinely hard part; use the design-first variant then.

inputs.name: Feature or bug to formalise
inputs.description: The change to make, ready to be turned into user stories and acceptance criteria.

inputs.name: Existing codebase context
inputs.description: The current architecture and code the design must fit into.

inputs.name: Spec-driven IDE or agent
inputs.description: Tooling such as Kiro that generates requirements, design, and a runnable task list.

outputs.name: Requirements document
outputs.description: User stories with EARS acceptance criteria that state, testably, what the system shall do.

outputs.name: Design document
outputs.description: Architecture, component interfaces, and data models, with diagrams, that satisfy the requirements.

outputs.name: Dependency-ordered task list
outputs.description: The design broken into tasks with their dependencies, ready to run independent ones in parallel.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  feat[Feature to formalise] --> req[Requirements in EARS]
  req --> design[Design: architecture + data models]
  design --> tasks[Dependency-ordered task list]
  tasks --> wave[Run tasks in dependency waves]
  wave --> check[Check against acceptance criteria]
  feat -.design-first variant.-> design

methodologies.example_scenario: A fintech team builds a transaction-export feature that must satisfy auditors, so vague requirements are a real risk. Using a spec-driven IDE in the Kiro shape, they started in the requirements phase. Each rule became a user story with EARS acceptance criteria, for example 'when an export includes transactions older than seven years, the system shall require a compliance approval'. Writing them this way surfaced two rules nobody had agreed on, which they resolved before any design. The tool then generated a design document with a sequence diagram, the export service interface, and the data model, which they checked against the requirements. It broke the design into fourteen tasks and built a dependency graph: schema and validation tasks had no dependencies and ran first, while the UI tasks waited on the service. They ran the independent tasks concurrently, then the dependent ones, and checked each finished task back against its acceptance criteria. The EARS phrasing made that check mechanical: every 'shall' was either demonstrably met or not.
methodologies.deep_dive: This workflow formalises the front of spec-driven development into three named artifacts. Requirements use EARS (Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax), which constrains each criterion to a 'when <trigger>, the system shall <response>' shape; the constraint is what makes requirements testable rather than aspirational. Design turns approved requirements into architecture, interfaces, and data models, typically with mermaid diagrams. Tasks decompose the design and carry an explicit dependency graph, so an executor can group independent tasks into waves and run them concurrently. The design-first variant inverts requirements and design: when the architecture is the hard constraint, you settle it first and then derive requirements that are guaranteed feasible against it. Compared with the Spec Kit shape of `spec-driven-development`, this workflow is heavier on testable acceptance criteria and explicit task dependencies, and lighter on a standing constitution, so the two are complementary rather than competing. The failure mode to watch is EARS theatre: criteria that read like 'shall' statements but are not actually checkable, which defeats the point of the phrasing.

steps.name: Gather requirements in EARS
steps.description: Write user stories with acceptance criteria in the form 'when X, the system shall Y'. The constraint forces each criterion to be checkable.

steps.name: Generate the design
steps.description: Turn approved requirements into a design document: architecture, interfaces, and data models, with diagrams, fitted to the existing code.

steps.name: Break into a dependency-ordered task list
steps.description: Decompose the design into tasks and record which depend on which, so independent work can run in parallel.

steps.name: Run tasks in waves
steps.description: Execute tasks by dependency wave: all tasks with no unmet dependencies run together, then the next wave once theirs are satisfied.

steps.name: Check against acceptance criteria
steps.description: Verify each finished task against its EARS criteria. Every 'shall' is either demonstrably met or it is not done.

principles: EARS phrasing earns its keep only if every 'shall' is actually checkable.
Requirements before design, unless the architecture is the hard part.
Make task dependencies explicit so independent work can run in parallel.
Acceptance criteria are the contract; check finished work against them.

known_failure_modes.note: Marking a task done because code exists, without checking it against its EARS acceptance criteria.
EARS theatre: criteria that read like 'shall' statements but are not actually checkable, so the workflow formalises nothing.

sources.title: Specs — Kiro IDE docs
sources.quote: Kiro uses the EARS format (Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax), which structures requirements as 'when you do X, the system shall do Y'.
